name: Jimmy Smith
abstract: James Oscar Smith (December 8, 1928 – February 8, 2005) was an American jazz musician who helped popularize the Hammond B-3 organ, creating a link between jazz and 1960s soul music. In 2005, Smith was awarded the NEA Jazz Masters Award from the National Endowment for the Arts, the highest honor that America bestows upon jazz musicians.

about_quotes.speaker: Fred Wesley
about_quotes.evidence: he’s a young man that plays organ like Jimmy Smith — I mean far, way, way better than Jimmy Smith.
about_quotes.doc_title: Fred Wesley interviewed by Monk Rowe, via Zoom, November 21, 2024

about_quotes.speaker: Kenny Burrell
about_quotes.evidence: I made so many, at least a dozen with Jimmy and then he made a couple with me under my name.
about_quotes.doc_title: Kenny Burrell — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Houston Person Part 1
about_quotes.evidence: I also worked with Jimmy Smith, who is the king, so I worked with a lot of them.
about_quotes.doc_title: Houston Person Part 1 interviewed by Monk Rowe and Michael Woods, Majesty of the Seas, May 29, 1995 [interview 1 of 2]

about_quotes.speaker: Lou Donaldson
about_quotes.evidence: You did so many gigs with Jimmy Smith in 1957 and 1958... We were compatible. He liked me.
about_quotes.doc_title: Lou Donaldson — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Pete Levin
about_quotes.evidence: I’d been listening to Jimmy Smith, like everybody, and trying to emulate him
about_quotes.doc_title: Pete Levin interviewed by Monk Rowe, Danbury, Connecticut, November 20, 2001

about_quotes.speaker: Ramsey Lewis
about_quotes.evidence: Interviewer mentions Jimmy Smith in the context of soul jazz; Interviewee responds by discussing Ray Charles as an influence, but never mentions Jimmy Smith directly or describes a personal/professional relationship with him.
about_quotes.doc_title: Ramsey Lewis — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History
